title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Runtime kernel selection caused latency spike (2026-03-18)
preview: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.
text: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.

title: How-to: Integrate Optimize with the Cost Model API (What-if projections + ROI)
preview:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-request payloads
- How to call the what-if projection endpoint(s) safely (bounds, timeouts, retries)
- How to interpret confidence intervals and communicate uncertainty
- Guardrails: when *not* to show ROI, and how to avoid over-optimistic recommendations

Optimize should treat the Cost Model API as the canonical source for cost primitives and projections. Avoid re-implementing cost math in Optimize except for UI presentation and product logic.

---

## Audience
- Optimize backend + recommendation pipeline engineers
- Applied ML (quantization / caching / batching ROI modeling)
- Product owners for recommendation UX

---

## Prerequisites
1) You have access to Cost Model API in the target environment (staging/prod).
2) You are using the shared client library:
- Package: `cost_model_client` (internal)
- Supports: retries, timeouts, schema version negotiation, request signing/tenancy headers
3) You understand the v1 taxonomy for breakdown categories (prefill vs decode, cache effects, overhead allocation).

If you need access to dashboards for validating results, see: confluence://eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/cost-model-api-observability

---

## Integration overview (Optimize flow)
Optimize typically runs projections in two places:
1) Recommendation generation (offline or nearline): compute ROI candidates, rank, and filter.
2) Recommendation rendering (online UI/API): optionally re-check ROI using the same baseline signature to avoid stale outputs.

Recommended pattern:
1) Build a **Baseline Workload Summary** (observed) for the customer + route/model + time window.
2) Build a **What-if Scenario** (config delta) for the recommendation.
3) Call Cost Model API **What-if Projection**.
4) Apply **guardrails** (confidence, sample size, policy bounds, quality constraints).
5) Store and display:
- Baseline cost estimate + range
- Projected cost estimate + range
- Delta (savings) + range
- Key drivers (batching efficiency uplift, cache hit-rate assumptions, quantization throughput factor)

---

## Endpoints used by Optimize (v1)
Optimize should use the what-if endpoint(s) for any “savings estimate”, not the raw estimate endpoint.

### 1) What-if projection
- Method: `POST`
- Path (internal): `/cost-model/v1/what-if`
- Purpose: Returns baseline vs projected cost (with breakdown + uncertainty) for a workload summary and scenario.

### 2) Optional: Baseline estimate only
- Method: `POST`
- Path (internal): `/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- Purpose: Only if you need a baseline estimate without running scenarios (generally not needed if you always call what-if).

Notes:
- Optimize must keep responses bounded: request is aggregated; response includes a small fixed set of breakdown categories.
- Do not request “per-request” projections. Use workload summaries.

---

## Versioning and schema negotiation
### Required headers (service-to-service)
- `X-Redwood-Tenant-Id: <tenant_uuid>`
- `X-Redwood-Project-Id: <project_uuid>` (if applicable)
- `X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version: v1` (pin for deterministic behavior)
- `X-Request-Id: <uuid>`

### Compatibility rules for Optimize
- Pin to `v1` unless explicitly rolling forward.
- Be strict on missing required fields you send.
- Be tolerant on *new fields in responses* (ignore unknown fields). The client library already does this.

---

## Constructing a Workload Summary (Optimize input)
The Cost Model API is designed to accept **aggregated workload summaries** that capture what matters for cost without high-cardinality request payloads.

### Choose a time window
Recommendations should use a time window that is long enough to stabilize:
- Minimum: 30 minutes
- Recommended: 6–24 hours (depending on traffic)

Avoid building ROI on very short windows unless you enforce strict guardrails (see “Guardrails” section).

### Recommended baseline grouping key
Use a grouping key that matches how your recommendation will apply:
- Tenant + project
- Route/workload identifier (Optimize concept)
- Model identifier + version
- Deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- Region (if relevant for cost)
- Quantization profile (baseline)

If you aggregate across too-broad groups, projections become misleading (e.g., mixing long-context and short-context traffic).

### Minimum fields (conceptual)
Your workload summary must represent:
1) Request volume and distribution
- `request_count`
- `time_window_seconds`
- `requests_per_second_avg` (or equivalent)

2) Token distributions
- Prompt tokens distribution (p50/p90/p99)
- Completion tokens distribution (p50/p90/p99)
- Total tokens observed (prompt + completion)

3) Runtime/serving context
- `model_id` (and model revision if applicable)
- `deployment_mode` (hosted | dedicated | private)
- `hardware_sku` (if known; otherwise allow Cost Model to infer via routing context)
- `streaming_enabled` (affects decode scheduling assumptions)

4) Caching + batching state
- Observed batch size distribution or effective batching factor
- Prefix/KV cache hit rate (if available) and whether caching is enabled

5) Overhead policy
- Pass `overhead_allocation_mode` as instructed by the platform defaults (generally “standard”). Optimize should not invent new overhead modes.

### Example: workload summary payload (illustrative)
Embed this structure in your request body using the client library types; field names may differ slightly depending on the generated SDK.

```
POST /cost-model/v1/what-if
X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version: v1

{
  "baseline": {
    "workload_summary": {
      "tenant_id": "tnt_9f2c...",
      "project_id": "prj_41a1...",
      "workload_key": "chat_support_widget",
      "time_window_seconds": 86400,
      "request_count": 128430,
      "model": {
        "model_id": "rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
        "model_revision": "2026-01-18",
        "quantization_profile_id": "fp16"
      },
      "deployment": {
        "mode": "hosted",
        "region": "us-east-1"
      },
      "tokens": {
        "prompt_tokens_p50": 410,
        "prompt_tokens_p90": 980,
        "prompt_tokens_p99": 3200,
        "completion_tokens_p50": 180,
        "completion_tokens_p90": 420,
        "completion_tokens_p99": 1200
      },
      "runtime_observations": {
        "streaming_enabled": true,
        "kv_cache_hit_rate": 0.22,
        "effective_batching_factor": 1.45
      },
      "overhead_allocation_mode": "standard"
    }
  },
  "scenario": {
    "scenario_id": "opt_rec_8f1b...",
    "change": {
      "type": "ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHE",
      "target_kv_cache_hit_rate": 0.45,
      "notes": "Enable prefix caching for stable system prompt + tools schema"
    }
  },
  "options": {
    "return_breakdown": true,
    "return_confidence": true,
    "max_breakdown_rows": 32
  }
}
```

Important: The Cost Model API may reject payloads that exceed bounds (see “Request bounds and performance”). Keep distributions compact (p50/p90/p99) and avoid sending full histograms unless explicitly allowed by schema.

---

## What-if scenarios supported (Optimize v1)
Optimize recommendations must map cleanly to supported scenario types. If you need a new scenario type, open an ENG ticket and coordinate with Platform + Applied ML before shipping.

### Supported scenario types (typical)
- Batching
  - Change target batching policy / effective batching factor
  - Constraints: bounded uplift; cannot exceed service-defined max for a given model/hardware

- Caching
  - Enable/disable prefix/KV caching
  - Provide a target cache hit rate (or let server infer from workload + policy)

- Quantization
  - Change `quantization_profile_id` (e.g., fp16 → int8)
  - Must include any required quality constraints flags (see “Quantization guardrails”)

- Routing / deployment mode (limited)
  - In v1, routing changes must be within approved policy envelopes; do not use what-if to “simulate pricing” for customer-facing quotes.

### Mapping recommendations → scenario fields
- Recommendation: “Increase batching by raising max batch tokens”
  - Scenario: `CHANGE_BATCHING_POLICY` with a bounded `target_effective_batching_factor`

- Recommendation: “Enable prefix caching for repeated system prompt”
  - Scenario: `ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHE` with `target_kv_cache_hit_rate`

- Recommendation: “Switch to int8 quantization”
  - Scenario: `CHANGE_QUANT_PROFILE` with `target_quantization_profile_id`

If a recommendation requires multiple changes, prefer:
- One combined scenario if schema supports it; otherwise
- Evaluate changes separately and show the most conservative savings (or block the rec until combined modeling exists)

---

## Interpreting results (cost, breakdown, confidence)
The Cost Model API returns:
- Baseline estimate
- Projected estimate
- Delta (absolute + percent)
- Breakdown categories
- Confidence / uncertainty (when requested)

### Confidence intervals
In v1, uncertainty is represented as quantiles (example):
- `point_estimate`
- `p10` and `p90` (or `low` and `high` bounds)
- `confidence_level` descriptor (e.g., “medium”)

Interpretation guidance for Optimize:
- Use the **range** (p10–p90) to decide whether to show ROI.
- If the range crosses zero savings (i.e., includes negative savings), treat the rec as **non-actionable** unless additional signals justify it.
- Do not claim a single deterministic savings number in UI copy. Use “Estimated savings” and show the range where feasible.

### Recommended UI/UX phrasing
- Preferred:
  - “Estimated savings: 8–14% (median 11%)”
- Avoid:
  - “You will save 12%”

### Breakdown categories
Optimize should not invent new breakdown labels. Use the taxonomy returned:
- Request-attributable compute (prefill)
- Request-attributable compute (decode)
- Cache-related adjustments (hits/misses, warm rate assumptions)
- Batching efficiency adjustments
- Overhead allocation (standard)

Optimize can optionally highlight “drivers” by taking the top 1–3 contributors to delta.

---

## Guardrails (required before showing ROI)
These guardrails are mandatory to prevent over-optimistic recommendations and support escalations (e.g., when customers compare Optimize vs Console vs invoice).

### 1) Minimum workload stability
Do not show ROI unless baseline meets at least one:
- `request_count >= 1,000` over the window, OR
- `time_window_seconds >= 6 hours` AND `request_count >= 300`

Rationale: low sample size drives noisy token distributions and unreliable cache/batching estimates.

### 2) Confidence gating
If `confidence_level == low` OR the returned interval is too wide:
- Hide ROI numbers (show “Insufficient data to estimate savings”)
- Or show only directional guidance (no %)

Suggested threshold:
- If `(p90 - p10) / max(point_estimate, epsilon) > 0.35`, treat as too uncertain.

### 3) Savings materiality threshold
Avoid spammy recommendations:
- Minimum percent savings: 5%
- Minimum absolute savings: $25/month equivalent (or tenant-tier specific threshold)

### 4) No “stacking” optimistic assumptions
If your recommendation uses an assumed target (e.g., cache hit rate), do not set it to a best-case value.
- Use conservative defaults unless the workload evidence supports higher values.
- If you have multiple plausible targets, run multiple what-if calls and take the conservative result.

### 5) Quantization guardrails (quality + eligibility)
Only show quantization ROI if ALL are true:
- Model catalog marks the target quant profile as “supported” for the model revision
- Customer/workload is eligible (e.g., not in a “no-loss” quality tier)
- Optimize has passing eval signals for the workload category (as defined by Applied ML)

If you cannot validate quality eligibility, show a “Potential savings” banner without numbers, or block the recommendation.

### 6) Rate limits and cost safety
Optimize must cap what-if calls per tenant to avoid runaway spend:
- Offline jobs: batch requests and dedupe by baseline signature
- Online: cache results (see below) and enforce per-tenant request ceilings

---

## Caching strategy (recommended)
What-if calls are usually repeatable for a given baseline signature and scenario.

### Cache key
- `tenant_id + project_id + workload_key + model_id + model_revision + baseline_quant_profile + scenario_type + scenario_params_hash + schema_version`

### TTL
- Offline jobs: cache for the job run (or 24h)
- Online UI rendering: 15–60 minutes depending on recommendation freshness

### Avoiding cache poisoning
- Include schema version and model revision in the key.
- If Cost Model API returns `telemetry_freshness_lag_seconds` above a threshold (if present), do not refresh cached values; keep last known-good.

---

## Request bounds and performance (Optimize responsibilities)
The Cost Model API enforces request bounds to protect latency and avoid cardinality blowups.

Optimize must:
- Send aggregated summaries (no per-request arrays)
- Avoid unbounded tag lists
- Limit breakdown rows via `max_breakdown_rows` (use 32 unless you have a proven need)
- Use timeouts appropriate for recommendation generation

Recommended client settings (service-to-service):
- Timeout: 750ms (online) / 2s (offline batch)
- Retries: 1 (online) / 2 (offline), with jitter
- Circuit breaking: enabled (use client library defaults)

If timeouts occur, degrade gracefully:
- Online: hide ROI (do not block the recommendation list)
- Offline: retry with backoff; if still failing, mark ROI as “unavailable” and continue pipeline

---

## Error handling and common responses
### 400 / INVALID_ARGUMENT
Causes:
- Scenario parameters out of allowed range
- Missing required workload fields

Optimize action:
- Treat as a bug; log request signature (not full payload with sensitive IDs beyond tenant/project)
- Suppress ROI for that recommendation and alert on error rate

### 409 / VERSION_MISMATCH (or similar)
Cause:
- Schema version negotiation issue

Optimize action:
- Pin to `v1` and update client library

### 429 / RATE_LIMITED
Cause:
- Too many what-if calls for a tenant or global budget

Optimize action:
- Use cached results where possible
- Backoff and avoid tight loops in offline generation

### 503 / DEPENDENCY_UNAVAILABLE
Cause:
- Telemetry lag / model catalog dependency issue

Optimize action:
- Hide ROI and display “temporarily unavailable”
- Do not invent numbers from legacy formulas (consistency is the goal)

---

## Validation checklist (before enabling in production)
1) Compare Optimize baseline cost (via Cost Model) vs Console baseline cost for the same workload key/time window.
- Expect small differences due to window alignment; investigate systematic deltas.

2) Sanity-check scenario directionality:
- Enabling cache should not increase cost unless hit rate assumption is near zero and overhead dominates.
- Quantization should not claim savings if the model/hardware combination is already throughput-bound elsewhere.

3) Verify guardrails in practice:
- Low-volume workloads should show “insufficient data” rather than wide or spiky ROI.

4) Track drift metrics (if available to you):
- Use the drift dashboard to ensure projections are not consistently optimistic.

---

## Rollout plan (Optimize)
1) Add feature flag:
- `optimize.cost_model_api.what_if.enabled`

2) Stage rollout:
- Staging → internal tenants → 5% prod → 25% → 100%

3) During rollout, record:
- Schema version distribution
- Error rate by scenario type
- Median and p90 confidence widths
- Customer-facing escalation rate (Support tags: ROI mismatch, invoice mismatch)

4) Rollback strategy:
- Disable ROI display first (keep recommendations)
- If needed, disable Cost Model calls entirely (fall back to “no ROI available” rather than legacy numbers)

---

## Troubleshooting
### “What-if savings seem too optimistic”
Most common root causes:
- Cache hit rate target set too high
- Workload summary mixes heterogeneous traffic (short + long context)
- Window too short (unstable token distributions)

Actions:
- Re-run with conservative targets
- Split workload keys
- Enforce minimum stability guardrails

### “Optimize numbers don’t match Console”
Check:
- Same time window alignment
- Same model revision and quant profile
- Same overhead allocation mode
- Same deployment mode/region

If still mismatched:
- Check Cost Model API schema version pinned
- Consult drift dashboard and file a ticket with sample signatures

---

## Contacts
- Cost Model API / Platform: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor
- Applied ML what-if methodology: Jonas Weber, Dr. Maya Srinivasan
- Telemetry inputs/data quality: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman
- Optimize product owner: Aditya Rao

For incidents, follow the service runbook referenced in the Cost Model service overview.

### 6) Rate limits and cost safety
Optimize must cap what-if calls per tenant to avoid runaway spend:
- Offline jobs: batch requests and dedupe by baseline signature
- Online: cache results (see below) and enforce per-tenant request ceilings

---

## Caching strategy (recommended)
What-if calls are usually repeatable for a given baseline signature and scenario.

### Cache key
- `tenant_id + project_id + workload_key + model_id + model_revision + baseline_quant_profile + scenario_type + scenario_params_hash + schema_version`

### TTL
- Offline jobs: cache for the job run (or 24h)
- Online UI rendering: 15–60 minutes depending on recommendation freshness

### Avoiding cache poisoning
- Include schema version and model revision in the key.
- If Cost Model API returns `telemetry_freshness_lag_seconds` above a threshold (if present), do not refresh cached values; keep last known-good.

---

## Request bounds and performance (Optimize responsibilities)
The Cost Model API enforces request bounds to protect latency and avoid cardinality blowups.

Optimize must:
- Send aggregated summaries (no per-request arrays)
- Avoid unbounded tag lists
- Limit breakdown rows via `max_breakdown_rows` (use 32 unless you have a proven need)
- Use timeouts appropriate for recommendation generation

Recommended client settings (service-to-service):
- Timeout: 750ms (online) / 2s (offline batch)
- Retries: 1 (online) / 2 (offline), with jitter
- Circuit breaking: enabled (use client library defaults)

If timeouts occur, degrade gracefully:
- Online: hide ROI (do not block the recommendation list)
- Offline: retry with backoff; if still failing, mark ROI as “unavailable” and continue pipeline

---

## Error handling and common responses
### 400 / INVALID_ARGUMENT
Causes:
- Scenario parameters out of allowed range
- Missing required workload fields

Optimize action:
- Treat as a bug; log request signature (not full payload with sensitive IDs beyond tenant/project)
- Suppress ROI for that recommendation and alert on error rate

### 409 / VERSION_MISMATCH (or similar)
Cause:
- Schema version negotiation issue

Optimize action:
- Pin to `v1` and update client library

### 429 / RATE_LIMITED
Cause:
- Too many what-if calls for a tenant or global budget

Optimize action:
- Use cached results where possible
- Backoff and avoid tight loops in offline generation

### 503 / DEPENDENCY_UNAVAILABLE
Cause:
- Telemetry lag / model catalog dependency issue

Optimize action:
- Hide ROI and display “temporarily unavailable”
- Do not invent numbers from legacy formulas (consistency is the goal)

---

## Validation checklist (before enabling in production)
1) Compare Optimize baseline cost (via Cost Model) vs Console baseline cost for the same workload key/time window.
- Expect small differences due to window alignment; investigate systematic deltas.

2) Sanity-check scenario directionality:
- Enabling cache should not increase cost unless hit rate assumption is near zero and overhead dominates.
- Quantization should not claim savings if the model/hardware combination is already throughput-bound elsewhere.

3) Verify guardrails in practice:
- Low-volume workloads should show “insufficient data” rather than wide or spiky ROI.

4) Track drift metrics (if available to you):
- Use the drift dashboard to ensure projections are not consistently optimistic.

---

## Rollout plan (Optimize)
1) Add feature flag:
- `optimize.cost_model_api.what_if.enabled`

2) Stage rollout:
- Staging → internal tenants → 5% prod → 25% → 100%

3) During rollout, record:
- Schema version distribution
- Error rate by scenario type
- Median and p90 confidence widths
- Customer-facing escalation rate (Support tags: ROI mismatch, invoice mismatch)

4) Rollback strategy:
- Disable ROI display first (keep recommendations)
- If needed, disable Cost Model calls entirely (fall back to “no ROI available” rather than legacy numbers)

---

## Troubleshooting
### “What-if savings seem too optimistic”
Most common root causes:
- Cache hit rate target set too high
- Workload summary mixes heterogeneous traffic (short + long context)
- Window too short (unstable token distributions)

Actions:
- Re-run with conservative targets
- Split workload keys
- Enforce minimum stability guardrails

### “Optimize numbers don’t match Console”
Check:
- Same time window alignment
- Same model revision and quant profile
- Same overhead allocation mode
- Same deployment mode/region

If still mismatched:
- Check Cost Model API schema version pinned
- Consult drift dashboard and file a ticket with sample signatures

---

## Contacts
- Cost Model API / Platform: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor
- Applied ML what-if methodology: Jonas Weber, Dr. Maya Srinivasan
- Telemetry inputs/data quality: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman
- Optimize product owner: Aditya Rao

For incidents, follow the service runbook referenced in the Cost Model service overview.

title: Runbook: Audit Log Coverage Regression (Missing Sensitive-Action Events)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s audit logging coverage regresses (e.g., a newly added/changed admin endpoint or workflow no longer emits a required audit event, or emits an event that fails schema/taxonomy requirements).

Coverage regressions are treated as security-impacting quality issues because they can break customer detection/investigation workflows and SOC2/ISO-aligned evidence expectations.

## When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when any of the following occurs:
- The Audit Coverage dashboard shows a drop in observed coverage for one or more `event_type`s.
- An alert fires for missing expected event types for a sensitive action.
- Support/Solutions reports a customer issue: “action happened, but we don’t see a corresponding audit event” (common in Private and Console workflows).
- CI begins failing due to new/changed code paths missing required audit fields or using an unregistered `event_type`.

If the issue is that events are being emitted but not delivered/exported (ingestion backlog, exporter failure, high drop rate), use the **Audit Log Pipeline Incident Runbook** instead.

---

## Definitions (quick)
- **Sensitive action:** An action listed in the sensitive actions taxonomy that requires an audit event (RBAC, credential lifecycle, policy/routing changes, retention/residency changes, audit export config, Private install/upgrade/rollback/config apply, etc.).
- **Coverage regression:** A sensitive action occurs (or is exercised by tests/synthetic) but the expected audit event is not observed OR is observed but invalid (wrong `event_type`, missing required fields, schema validation failure).
- **Producer:** The service/tool that emits the audit event (API Gateway, Control Plane, Console backend, Private installer, policy engine, exporter normalization layer).

---

## Severity and handling guidelines
### Default severity
- **SEV2 (security-impacting)** if:
  - A required sensitive-action audit event is missing in any production tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) for GA surfaces, OR
  - The regression affects multiple tenants/projects, OR
  - A customer SIEM export pipeline is known to be affected.

- **SEV3** if:
  - The missing event is in a gated/feature-flagged preview surface, OR
  - Impact is limited to internal environments / pre-production.

### Escalation triggers (page/incident)
Escalate to on-call / incident process when:
- Coverage gap coincides with pipeline symptoms (ingestion latency spike, drop-rate alerts).
- The missing event is for high-risk actions (RBAC changes, API key create/rotate/revoke, audit export destination changes, retention/residency changes).
- A regulated enterprise customer is blocked (Support ticket tagged: “security review”, “SOC2 evidence”, “Private”).

Primary escalation channel: `#eng-security` and `#eng-platform`. For incident coordination: `#incidents`.

---

## Required access
- Observability dashboards (audit coverage + event validation failures).
- Ability to query audit events (by `event_type`, `service_name`, `deployment_mode`, `tenant_id`, `request_id`).
- Read access to service repos / CI logs (for lint failures and test output).
- Linear/Jira access for creating/triaging work items.

Owners to contact if access is blocked:
- Dashboard access / telemetry: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman
- SRE / alerts: Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes
- CI enforcement tooling: Sahana Iyer

---

## Quick decision tree
1) Are audit events being emitted but not delivered/exported?
- Yes → Use **Audit Log Pipeline Incident Runbook** (delivery issue).
- No / unknown → Continue.

2) Is coverage drop limited to a single `deployment_mode` (e.g., Private only)?
- Yes → Likely producer parity gap (Console/Private installer/control plane differences).
- No → Likely shared library/schema/taxonomy change or a producer regression across modes.

3) Does CI/lint fail for audit logging in the relevant repo?
- Yes → Likely missing required fields or unregistered `event_type`. Fix in code + add test.
- No → Likely code path exists but event emission not called or behind an unexpected branch/permission check.

---

## Triage checklist (do these in order)
### Step 1 — Identify exactly what is missing
Capture the following (paste into the ticket/incident):
- Expected `event_type` (from taxonomy)
- Action/workflow that should generate it (endpoint + UI path + installer command)
- Affected deployment mode(s): Hosted / Dedicated / Private
- First observed time (approx) and whether it correlates with a deploy
- Example tenant/project (if safe) and the actor type (user, service account, system)

Common signals:
- Coverage dashboard tile: “Expected vs Observed by event_type (24h)”
- Alert name: `audit_coverage_gap_detected` or `missing_sensitive_action_events`
- CI failure name: `audit-event-required-fields` or `event-type-registry-check`

### Step 2 — Confirm it’s a coverage regression (not an ingestion delay)
Run these sanity checks:
- Check audit ingestion latency for the same window.
- Check drop-rate metrics and exporter error rate.
- Check whether other unrelated event types are flowing normally.

Rule of thumb:
- If **many event types** drop simultaneously and ingestion latency spikes → delivery issue.
- If **one/few event types** drop while overall volume is steady → coverage regression.

### Step 3 — Reproduce (preferred) or isolate via traces
Pick the lowest-risk environment where you can reproduce:
1. Staging or dogfood tenant
2. A private test fixture environment (if Private-only)

Reproduce the sensitive action and capture:
- `request_id`
- `trace_id`
- `admin_action_id` (if present)
- HTTP route + method
- Actor identity context (user_id / service_account_id / impersonation)

Then search audit events by correlation id (example query patterns):
- `request_id:<id> AND event_type:*`
- `admin_action_id:<id> AND schema_version:v1*`
- `trace_id:<id> AND service_name:<service>`

Expected outcomes:
- No audit event at all → missing emission.
- Audit event exists but invalid → schema/taxonomy mismatch.
- Audit event exists but wrong actor/target semantics → producer bug (still considered a coverage failure for acceptance).

### Step 4 — Identify the producer and route to the right owner
Use the workflow to map to a producing system:
- Control Plane (tenant config, RBAC, API keys, policy/rollouts): **Ethan Park** (lead), Benji Okafor
- API Gateway (admin endpoints, quota overrides, auth policy enforcement knobs): **Amara Diallo**
- Console backend (admin workflows, export config UI/BE): **Dylan Brooks / Chloe Martin**
- Policy engine (routing/policy changes): **Jordan Lee**
- Private installer/upgrade tooling: **Vivek Kulkarni** (PM), engineering owner via `redwood-private-installer` rotation; ping `#eng` with “private-installer” tag
- Exporter normalization/schema tagging: **Kevin Osei**
- Telemetry coverage dashboard/alerts: **Logan Wright**
- CI enforcement/lints: **Sahana Iyer**

Routing instructions:
- Create a Linear ticket under **Engineering / Security & Platform**.
- Add label: `audit-logging` + the relevant subsystem label (`control-plane`, `console`, `gateway`, `private-installer`, `exporter`, `policy-engine`).
- Assign directly if clear; otherwise post in `#eng-platform` with the captured repro + correlation IDs and request an owner within 4 business hours.

### Step 5 — Correlate with a change
If possible, tie the regression to:
- A specific deploy (service release timestamp)
- A PR merged recently touching:
  - auth middleware
  - routing changes
  - RBAC checks
  - new endpoints
  - refactors around request context propagation

If you find the likely PR, link it in the ticket and tag the author + service owner.

---

## Mitigation options
Choose based on impact and feasibility.

### Option A — Hotfix to emit the event (preferred)
- Add the missing audit event emission on the sensitive code path.
- Validate against schema v1 using the shared schema validator.
- Ensure the `event_type` is registered in the central registry.
- Add unit/integration tests (see “Adding enforcement tests”).

### Option B — Emergency rollback
If a deploy is clearly responsible and impact is high:
- Roll back the offending service release.
- Confirm coverage returns to baseline.
- Open follow-up ticket for forward fix (do not rely on rollback long-term).

### Option C — Temporary exception (last resort)
If immediate fix is not possible and risk is understood:
- File an exception request per the audit logging exception process.
- Include explicit expiration (default max: 14 days) and mitigation (manual review, added monitoring).
- Note: Exceptions are not acceptable for the highest-risk actions without Marcus Lin + Naomi Feldman approval.

---

## Verification (what “fixed” means)
A fix is complete when ALL are true:
- The sensitive action reliably emits the expected `event_type` in the relevant deployment mode(s).
- The event validates against schema v1 (no required-field omissions).
- Actor attribution is correct (including impersonation context where applicable).
- Correlation fields are present (`request_id` + `trace_id`, and `admin_action_id` when produced by Console/admin workflows).
- Coverage dashboard shows the event type returning to baseline and no “expected vs observed” gaps for at least one full deploy cycle.
- Regression prevention test(s) are merged and passing in CI.

For customer-reported issues:
- Provide Support with:
  - the `event_type`
  - the fixed version/build
  - a short confirmation statement that duplicates are possible due to at-least-once delivery and should be deduped by `event_id` (if relevant)

---

## Adding enforcement tests (how to prevent recurrence)
### 1) Register or confirm `event_type` in the central registry
- If a new sensitive action was introduced, add its `event_type` to the registry.
- Ensure required fields match taxonomy requirements (e.g., actor + target + diff summary for config changes).

### 2) Add unit tests at the producer
Minimum:
- Test that the code path calls audit emitter with:
  - correct `event_type`
  - non-empty `event_id`
  - `schema_version` set to `v1` or `v1.x`
  - required fields present (actor, target/resource, timestamp, service_name, deployment_mode, tenancy fields)

### 3) Add integration/E2E tests for the sensitive action
Use the standard harness (see Linear ENG-4834) to:
- Execute the sensitive action via API or UI-backend call.
- Poll the audit sink/test collector.
- Assert an event exists with the expected `event_type` and validated schema.

Recommended assertions (keep stable to avoid flakes):
- Must match: `event_type`, `service_name`, `deployment_mode`, `actor.type`, presence of `request_id`.
- Avoid brittle assertions on dynamic text. Prefer structured diff summaries when available.

### 4) Ensure CI coverage gates are updated
If the regression came from a new endpoint/workflow:
- Add the endpoint/action to the “sensitive actions coverage list” used by CI gates (where applicable).
- Confirm the audit lints are triggered on the owning repo (ENG-4831 patterns).

### 5) Update coverage expectations (telemetry)
If dashboards track “expected vs observed” from a static list:
- Add the new expected `event_type` so a future missing event pages early.
- Owner: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman.

---

## Common root causes and fixes
- **New admin endpoint added without instrumentation** → add audit emitter call + test.
- **Refactor bypassed middleware that injects actor/source_ip/correlation** → restore context propagation; add regression test for actor + `request_id`.
- **Console workflow calls a backend mutation that isn’t classified as sensitive** → update taxonomy and ensure backend emits the event (do not rely on frontend-only logging).
- **Private installer parity gap** (hosted emits, private doesn’t) → instrument installer command execution and capture operator identity where available; ensure schema alignment.
- **Event emitted but dropped by validator** (missing required field) → fix producer payload; ensure redaction rules don’t blank required fields.

---

## Communication templates
### Slack (internal)
Post in `#eng-security` and `#eng-platform`:
- “Coverage regression detected: missing `event_type=<...>` for action <...> in <deployment_mode>. First seen <time>. Repro request_id=<...> trace_id=<...>. Suspected producer=<service>. Linear=<link>.”

### Support handoff
- “Identified missing audit event for <action>. Fix merged in <service>/<version>. Expected `event_type=<...>` will be present with `schema_version=v1`. If customers see duplicates during retries, dedupe by `event_id`.”

---

## Owner checklist (post-incident / post-fix)
- [ ] Add/confirm E2E coverage test for the workflow.
- [ ] Ensure event type is in registry + taxonomy.
- [ ] Confirm dashboards/alerts include the new event type if applicable.
- [ ] If customer-impacting: add a brief entry to the internal release notes and link the Support ticket.

---

## Appendix: What to include in the Linear ticket
Title:
- “Audit coverage regression: missing `<event_type>` for `<workflow>` in `<mode>`”

Description:
- Expected event_type + taxonomy link
- Steps to reproduce
- Correlation IDs (`request_id`, `trace_id`, `admin_action_id`)
- Observed behavior (no event / invalid event)
- Scope (which tenants/modes) and severity
- Suspected owning service and recent changes

Attachments:
- Redacted sample audit events around the repro window (do not include secrets; follow redaction rules)

---

## Changelog
- 2026-02-24: Added enforcement test section and clarified routing table for Private installer and exporter normalization.
- 2026-02-10: Updated severity guidance and correlation ID capture requirements.
- 2026-01-31: Initial published runbook.

## Communication templates
### Slack (internal)
Post in `#eng-security` and `#eng-platform`:
- “Coverage regression detected: missing `event_type=<...>` for action <...> in <deployment_mode>. First seen <time>. Repro request_id=<...> trace_id=<...>. Suspected producer=<service>. Linear=<link>.”

### Support handoff
- “Identified missing audit event for <action>. Fix merged in <service>/<version>. Expected `event_type=<...>` will be present with `schema_version=v1`. If customers see duplicates during retries, dedupe by `event_id`.”

---

## Owner checklist (post-incident / post-fix)
- [ ] Add/confirm E2E coverage test for the workflow.
- [ ] Ensure event type is in registry + taxonomy.
- [ ] Confirm dashboards/alerts include the new event type if applicable.
- [ ] If customer-impacting: add a brief entry to the internal release notes and link the Support ticket.

---

## Appendix: What to include in the Linear ticket
Title:
- “Audit coverage regression: missing `<event_type>` for `<workflow>` in `<mode>`”

Description:
- Expected event_type + taxonomy link
- Steps to reproduce
- Correlation IDs (`request_id`, `trace_id`, `admin_action_id`)
- Observed behavior (no event / invalid event)
- Scope (which tenants/modes) and severity
- Suspected owning service and recent changes

Attachments:
- Redacted sample audit events around the repro window (do not include secrets; follow redaction rules)

---

## Changelog
- 2026-02-24: Added enforcement test section and clarified routing table for Private installer and exporter normalization.
- 2026-02-10: Updated severity guidance and correlation ID capture requirements.
- 2026-01-31: Initial published runbook.

title: Cost Model Service (cost-model-api) — Service Overview
preview:
## Summary
Cost Model Service (service name: **cost-model-api**) provides a single, versioned implementation for inference cost estimation and cost breakdowns across Redwood products.

It is the canonical source for:
- **Per-request cost estimates** and standardized breakdown taxonomy (prefill/decode, cache, batch efficiency, overhead allocation, quantization deltas)
- **Aggregated cost breakdowns** for Console dashboards (bounded-cardinality responses)
- **What-if projections** used by Optimize to quantify ROI from configuration changes (batching/caching/quantization)

This document describes architecture, dependencies, SLOs, ownership/on-call, and rollout strategy.

---

## Service owners
**Primary owner team:** Platform Control Plane
- Service/API lead: Ethan Park
- Control plane integration: Benji Okafor, Cole Summers
- Auth/tenancy: Kaitlyn Nguyen

**Reliability partner:** SRE
- Reliability lead: Rafael Mendes

**Key dependencies owners:**
- Telemetry & Analytics: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman
- Runtime metrics/instrumentation: Sofia Ivanova, Omar El-Sayed, Grace Kim
- Finance alignment: Laura Bennett, Rishi Malhotra

---

## Where this service sits
The Cost Model Service is an internal service + shared library that standardizes cost calculations. It is intentionally *not* the billing system of record.

### Primary callers
- **Redwood Console backend** (hot path for cost breakdown pages; calls are cacheable)
- **Redwood Optimize backend** (what-if projections; heavier compute but guarded and bounded)
- **Finance/analytics pipelines** (offline reconciliations and reporting windows; typically via batch exports or aggregated endpoints)

---

## High-level architecture (text diagram)

```
            +-----------------------+
            |  Redwood Console BE   |
            |  (dashboards, APIs)   |
            +-----------+-----------+
                        |
                        | (internal, mTLS)
                        v
+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|                cost-model-api                 |
|----------------------------------------------|
|  1) Request validation + tenancy/authz        |
|  2) Version negotiation (schema + semantics)  |
|  3) Cost computation engine                   |
|     - cost breakdown taxonomy                 |
|     - overhead allocation policy              |
|     - quantization profile deltas             |
|  4) Response bounding + aggregation guards    |
|  5) Cache (read-through) for hot keys         |
+-----------+-------------------+---------------+
            |                   |
            |                   |
            v                   v
  +------------------+   +---------------------+
  | Telemetry Store  |   | Model Catalog       |
  | (token + runtime |   | (model + quant      |
  |  metrics, lag)   |   |  profile metadata)  |
  +------------------+   +---------------------+
            |
            v
  +------------------+
  | Drift Monitor Job|
  | (scheduled; SLI) |
  +------------------+
            |
            v
  +------------------+
  | Observed Spend / |
  | Billing Primitives|
  +------------------+

            +-----------------------+
            |   Redwood Optimize    |
            | (recommendations/ROI) |
            +-----------+-----------+
                        |
                        v
                 (what-if endpoints)
```

Notes:
- **Console** calls are latency-sensitive; the service is designed to be cache-friendly and returns bounded responses.
- **Optimize** calls can be more compute-heavy, but are constrained by request guardrails (limits on parameter ranges, cardinality, and time horizon).
- A **drift monitoring job** compares predicted vs observed spend to produce a correctness/drift SLI.

---

## Dependencies

### Required upstream data
1) **Telemetry Store** (authoritative inputs for per-request and aggregated cost)
- Token accounting: prompt/prefill tokens, decode tokens, output tokens
- Cache metrics: KV cache hit/miss rates and bytes (when available), cache eligibility flags
- Scheduling metrics: effective batch size, queue time, batching window outcomes
- Runtime stage breakdown: prefill vs decode wall time and/or token accounting split

Owners: Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman)

2) **Model Catalog**
- Model identifiers, versions, architecture class
- Hardware compatibility metadata
- **Quantization profile IDs** and relevant metadata used by the cost model

Owner: Platform / Model Catalog maintainers (in coordination with Applied ML)

3) **Overhead allocation policy**
- Finance-approved policy to allocate shared costs to request-attributable vs overhead buckets
- Reporting windows and reconciliation rules

Owners: Finance/FP&A (Laura Bennett, Rishi Malhotra) + Platform

### Downstream integrations
- Console cost breakdown views (feature flag gated)
- Optimize ROI estimates and recommendation guardrails
- Billing hooks alignment (shared primitives; may intentionally differ for invoicing semantics; see ADRs)

---

## Data flow & request types

### Request types supported (v1)
- **Estimate**: Per-request estimated cost + breakdown (for request detail / tracing contexts)
- **Breakdown**: Aggregated cost breakdown over a bounded query window (for dashboards)
- **What-if**: Projected deltas based on configuration changes (for Optimize)

### Query bounding (hard requirements)
To prevent cardinality and latency blowups, the service enforces:
- Maximum time window per request (e.g., dashboard query windows must be bounded; long-range queries require pre-aggregation)
- Maximum number of group-by dimensions
- Maximum response size (rows and bytes)
- Allowed parameter ranges for what-if (e.g., batch size multipliers, cache warm-rate assumptions, quantization profile sets)

The exact limits are maintained in the API standards addendum and service config.

---

## Cost computation model (implementation overview)
The cost-model-api combines three layers:

1) **Standard Cost Spec (schema + semantics)**
- Versioned schemas for inputs/outputs (e.g., cost_model_spec v1)
- Explicit semantics for:
  - Prefill vs decode attribution
  - Cache hits vs misses vs ineligible requests
  - Batch efficiency and queue-time effects
  - Quantization profile deltas (throughput/memory impacts; quality guardrails)
  - Overhead allocation buckets

2) **Deterministic computation engine**
- Pure functions over canonical inputs where possible
- Stable rounding rules and currency/unit conventions
- Explicit “unknown/unavailable” handling for missing telemetry fields (no silent backfill that changes semantics)

3) **Confidence/uncertainty for projections (What-if only)**
- What-if returns a point estimate plus a confidence indicator when driven by empirically learned parameters
- Guardrails prevent projections when input uncertainty is too high or when inputs are out of policy bounds

---

## SLAs / SLOs (service-level)
Detailed SLO definitions live in the SLO page (see related pages). This section summarizes the operational targets used by Platform + SRE.

### Availability SLO
- **99.9%** monthly availability for core endpoints used by Console
- Degraded-mode behavior is allowed (see “Fallbacks”)

### Latency SLO (p95)
- Estimate/Breakdown endpoints (Console path): **p95 < 250ms** for cache-hit and **p95 < 600ms** for cache-miss under typical dashboard query bounds
- What-if endpoint (Optimize path): **p95 < 2.0s** for bounded scenarios

### Correctness / Drift SLI
- A scheduled drift job compares predicted costs (from cost-model primitives) to observed spend attribution over an agreed reporting window.
- Alerting is based on drift thresholds that account for telemetry lag and overhead allocation windows.

### Error budget policy
- If latency or errors threaten Console UX, the Console integration must automatically fall back to the legacy breakdown path (flag-controlled), and SRE may request a rollout pause.

---

## Reliability: fallbacks and failure modes

### Client fallbacks (required)
- Console must implement:
  - Timeouts (short)
  - Retries (bounded)
  - Fallback behavior (legacy cost breakdown implementation) behind a feature flag

- Optimize must implement:
  - Timeouts (longer)
  - No unbounded retries
  - Safe default behavior (suppress recommendation / show “insufficient confidence”) when the what-if endpoint is unavailable

### Common failure modes
- **Telemetry lag / partial ingestion** → drift alerts, missing fields, inconsistent breakdowns
- **Schema mismatch / version negotiation errors** → 4xx from service; clients should downgrade gracefully
- **Cardinality explosion** from overly broad group-bys → request rejected with explicit error code
- **Cache stampede** on common dashboard keys → mitigated via request coalescing + short TTL caching

---

## Deployment & environments
- Environments: dev, staging, prod
- Deployed as a standard internal control-plane service with:
  - mTLS service-to-service auth
  - Tenant-aware authorization checks
  - Per-route rate limits (stricter for what-if)

Config highlights:
- Cache TTLs are endpoint-specific
- Feature flags control:
  - Console adoption percentage
  - Optimize what-if enablement
  - Strictness of schema enforcement (e.g., rejecting vs warning on unknown fields)

---

## On-call & escalation

### On-call rotation
- **Primary:** Platform Control Plane on-call (cost-model-api)
- **Secondary:** SRE on-call (platform services)

### Escalation
- Telemetry ingestion incidents: page Telemetry & Analytics on-call
- Runtime instrumentation regressions: page Runtime Scheduling/Kernels on-call
- Finance reconciliation concerns (non-urgent): route to FP&A partner (Rishi Malhotra) via ticket

### Operational readiness requirements
Before enabling Console in production beyond initial canary:
- Dashboards exist for latency, error rates, request volume, version distribution, and drift
- Runbook is current and linked in service alerts
- Client fallback is verified in staging with forced fault injection

---

## Observability
Metrics emitted by cost-model-api:
- Request volume by endpoint + tenant tier (bounded cardinality)
- Latency (p50/p95/p99) by endpoint
- Error rates by error class (validation, auth, upstream dependency, compute timeout)
- Cache hit ratio (endpoint-level)
- Schema version distribution (requested vs served)
- Drift metrics (from scheduled job) and telemetry lag indicators

Logging/tracing:
- Trace IDs propagated from Console/Optimize
- Logs must not include raw prompts or customer content; only derived counts and stable identifiers

---

## Rollout strategy

### Phase 0 — Service bootstrap (completed)
- Ship service skeleton, auth/tenancy, and schema package
- Implement bounded endpoints with deterministic behavior

### Phase 1 — Staging integrations
- Console integration behind flag with fallback enabled
- Optimize integration behind flag for internal tenants only
- Validate schema version negotiation behavior

### Phase 2 — Production canary
- Enable for a small set of internal tenants and low-risk customers
- Monitor: latency, errors, schema version distribution, and drift SLI
- Compare Console breakdowns vs legacy for a sample of dashboard queries

### Phase 3 — Expand adoption
- Increase Console coverage gradually (e.g., 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
- Expand Optimize what-if access with strict guardrails
- Align billing hooks to consume the same primitives (document intentional differences)

### Rollback plan
- Immediate rollback via feature flag to legacy breakdowns (Console)
- Disable what-if endpoint exposure (Optimize) and freeze new recommendation rollouts
- If drift alerts are driven by telemetry lag: widen lag tolerance window temporarily and open follow-up work with Telemetry

---

## Open items / follow-ups
- Finalize drift thresholds by deployment mode (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
- Reduce dependency on high-cardinality telemetry dimensions for dashboard queries (prefer pre-aggregations)
- Document a clear customer-support explanation template for “Console vs invoice differences” rooted in the standard taxonomy and reporting windows

---

## Appendix: operational checklist (quick)
- [ ] Pager routes to Platform Control Plane on-call
- [ ] Dashboards present in Grafana (latency/errors/version/drift)
- [ ] Alerts link to runbook
- [ ] Client timeouts + fallback verified
- [ ] Request bounds tested (reject oversized queries)
- [ ] Schema version negotiation tested across Console/Optimize client library versions

title: ADR-021: Standardize Cost Model Interfaces (Canonical Spec + Cost Model API)
preview:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names.
- Billing/quota hooks use yet another set of primitives and accounting splits.

This has led to:
- Inconsistent customer-facing numbers (Console vs invoice) and inconsistent internal reporting.
- Unclear semantics for “what counts” (prefill vs decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, quantization impact, and overhead).
- Duplicated engineering effort and fragile integrations when any one surface evolves.
- Operational risk: drift investigations are slowed by lack of a single “source of truth” representation.

We need to unify inputs/outputs so Console (dashboards/observability), Optimize (recommendations + ROI), and finance/unit-economics reporting use the same cost primitives and semantics.

## Decision
We will define and adopt a single canonical, versioned cost model specification (schema + semantics) and expose it through a stable internal Cost Model API (service + client library). Console, Optimize, telemetry aggregation, and billing hooks will migrate to consume the same spec/API (with explicitly documented exceptions where required for invoicing/legal constraints).

### What this means concretely
1) **Canonical spec**
- A versioned schema package in the monorepo defines canonical inputs and outputs.
- The spec includes required semantics (units, attribution rules, determinism expectations, and bounds).
- Changes are gated by a review process and compatibility rules.

2) **Single internal API**
- A Cost Model API provides:
  - Per-request cost estimate and breakdown.
  - Aggregated breakdowns (bounded cardinality).
  - “What-if” projections for proposed config changes (batching, caching, quantization).
- Console and Optimize call the API via a shared client library with version negotiation.

3) **Governance and ownership**
- **Owner team:** Platform Control Plane (service/API), with cross-functional reviewers from Telemetry & Analytics, Console, Optimize/Applied ML, Runtime Scheduling/Kernels, SRE, and Finance.
- Schema changes require an ADR or a formal design review depending on impact.

## Decision Drivers
- **Consistency:** A single representation prevents mismatched cost definitions across product surfaces.
- **Auditability:** Finance requires stable, versioned semantics that can be referenced for reporting windows.
- **Velocity:** Removing duplicated logic enables faster iteration on cost breakdown UI and Optimize modeling.
- **Operational reliability:** Drift detection, incident response, and telemetry integrity checks require a unified contract.
- **Performance constraints:** Console hot paths require bounded response sizes, caching strategies, and predictable latency.

## Alternatives Considered
### A1) Per-product implementations with “best effort” alignment
Each surface (Console, Optimize, billing hooks) keeps its own cost logic and only aligns via documentation.
- Pros:
  - Lowest short-term engineering change.
  - Teams can iterate independently.
- Cons:
  - High likelihood of semantic divergence.
  - Continued customer trust issues (Console vs invoice).
  - Drift debugging remains slow; no single ground truth.
  - Duplicate instrumentation and computation.

Decision: Rejected.

### A2) Shared library only (no service)
Define a shared cost computation library; Console and Optimize link it and run computations locally.
- Pros:
  - Fewer runtime dependencies; avoids service availability concerns.
  - Simple for batch/offline use-cases.
- Cons:
  - Hard to centralize caching, bounds, and policy guardrails.
  - Version skew across deploys is likely (especially across many services).
  - Harder to enforce consistent configuration and data quality validation.

Decision: Rejected as the primary interface. A shared library will exist, but as a client + optional local evaluator for tests/fixtures.

### A3) Telemetry-as-source-of-truth only
Compute costs exclusively in the telemetry pipeline and have Console/Optimize read precomputed tables.
- Pros:
  - Efficient for aggregated dashboards.
  - Centralizes computation.
- Cons:
  - Doesn’t address per-request and interactive what-if needs.
  - Tighter coupling to telemetry latency and backfill complexity.
  - Optimize needs projection modeling beyond observed telemetry.

Decision: Rejected as the sole approach; telemetry remains a key input and validation layer.

## Scope and Non-Goals
### In scope
- Canonical schema for cost inputs/outputs and taxonomy alignment.
- Internal Cost Model API endpoints for estimate/breakdown/what-if.
- Standardized primitives shared by Console, Optimize, and billing hooks.
- Compatibility and deprecation policy.

### Out of scope (initial phase)
- Replacing billing/RevRec systems as the system of record.
- Building customer-facing pricing calculators.
- Perfect reconciliation to invoices in all historical edge cases; we will explicitly document known deltas where required (e.g., invoicing rounding, contractual minimums).

## Key Trade-offs and Rationale
### Centralization vs independence
- Centralization reduces inconsistency and improves auditability, but introduces a dependency on a shared service and governance.
- We accept this trade-off because cost definitions are cross-cutting and must be consistent.

### Service dependency vs performance
- A synchronous service in Console hot paths can add latency risk.
- We mitigate via:
  - Aggressive response bounding and pagination rules.
  - Client-side caching guidance and server-side memoization.
  - SLOs and an explicit degraded-mode fallback for Console (see Migration Plan).

### Determinism vs probabilistic modeling (what-if)
- Optimize projections may require uncertainty estimates.
- We will return deterministic point estimates plus optional confidence bands when supported, and clearly flag when uncertainty is high.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Console and Optimize share identical field names, semantics, and breakdown categories.
- Finance can reference a schema version for reporting periods, improving audit trails.
- Drift monitoring can be defined against the same primitives.
- Reduced duplicated work and fewer “definition debates” during incidents.

### Negative / Risks
- Schema governance can slow iteration if not streamlined.
- API availability/performance becomes critical for downstream consumers.
- Migration complexity: multiple existing pipelines and consumers must be updated.

### Risk mitigations
- Introduce feature flags and fallback behavior for Console integration.
- Publish clear SLOs and oncall ownership.
- Provide fixture datasets and golden tests to validate compatibility.
- Enforce request bounds and guardrails for what-if to prevent expensive computations.

## Ownership and Governance
### Owners
- **Primary:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park).
- **Telemetry input contracts:** Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright).
- **Runtime-exported inputs:** Runtime Scheduling/Kernels (Sofia Ivanova, Omar El-Sayed, Grace Kim).
- **SLOs and incident process:** SRE (Rafael Mendes).
- **Unit economics alignment:** Finance/FP&A (Laura Bennett, Rishi Malhotra).

### Change process
- Backward-incompatible changes require:
  - New major schema version and published migration guidance.
  - Deprecation window aligned to reporting windows (minimum 90 days unless approved by Finance + Platform).
- Backward-compatible additions are allowed at minor versions.
- All schema changes must include:
  - Updated docs of semantics.
  - Golden fixture updates (or explicit rationale).
  - Version distribution monitoring plan.

## Migration Plan (Phased)
### Phase 0: Inventory and alignment
- Inventory current cost fields, definitions, and known deltas.
- Lock v1 taxonomy and semantics (see ADR-022).

### Phase 1: Define schema v1 + service skeleton
- Publish cost-model schema package v1.
- Stand up Cost Model API with auth/tenancy, request bounds, and basic endpoints.

### Phase 2: Wire telemetry and billing primitives
- Formalize telemetry inputs and data quality checks.
- Align billing hooks to standardized primitives; explicitly document any invoicing-specific adjustments.

### Phase 3: Console integration behind feature flag
- Console calls Cost Model API for breakdown views.
- Fallback behavior (degraded mode):
  - If API is unavailable or exceeds latency budget, Console may fall back to last-known cached aggregates or disable the most granular breakdown sections.
  - All fallbacks emit metrics and are visible in dashboards.

### Phase 4: Optimize integration
- Optimize uses what-if endpoints and standard breakdown outputs for ROI.
- Add guardrails for what-if parameter ranges and returned result size.

### Phase 5: Launch + enforcement
- Turn on by default for internal consumers, then expand rollout.
- Freeze legacy cost computation paths and deprecate after the agreed window.

## Success Criteria
- Console and Optimize display consistent totals and breakdown categories for the same request cohort (within accepted tolerance).
- Drift SLI defined and tracked; anomalies actionable with documented runbook.
- Billing hooks consume standard primitives with documented, minimal differences.
- Version distribution monitored; no long-lived stragglers on deprecated versions.

## Open Questions
- Which overhead components must be present in v1 outputs vs v2 (and at what aggregation level) while keeping responses bounded?
- Final policy for when to attribute queue time and “idle” capacity overhead at request level vs pool/time window.
- How to represent quantization uncertainty in what-if outputs without encouraging overconfident savings estimates.

## References
- PRD: Standard Cost Model Spec + API
- ADR-022: Cost breakdown taxonomy and semantics
- Cost Model API v1 design spec
- Cost Model API guidelines (naming, bounds, determinism expectations)
- Service overview + SLO/runbooks (to be linked as they are published)
